metadata.provenance: Synthetic dataset generated via seeded procedural noise to emulate plausible 2016–2026 anomaly trends; not derived from real satellite records.

2016.description: One of the warmest years on record due to an exceptionally strong El Niño event, driving heavy precipitation in Pacific coastal regions and dry anomalies in Southeast Asia.

2017.description: Transition to a neutral state with weak cooling in the eastern Pacific. Characterized by severe wildfire seasons in Western North America and Southern Europe.

2018.description: La Niña conditions bring wetter conditions to Australia and parts of Brazil, with cooler global surface temperatures relative to recent years.

2019.description: Weak warm phase leads to significant droughts in Australia (driving the Black Summer bushfires) and warm temperature records in Africa and Europe.

2020.description: A moderate La Niña develops. Despite Pacific cooling, global temperatures remain high, matching 2016. High tropical cyclone activity in the Atlantic.

2021.description: Double-dip La Niña brings cool sea surface temperatures in the equatorial Pacific, intense rainfall in Australia and Southeast Asia, and cold waves in Texas.

2022.description: Rare third consecutive year of La Niña conditions. Heavy flooding hits Pakistan and East Australia, while extreme droughts worsen in the Horn of Africa.

2023.description: Rapid development of a strong El Niño. Global temperatures smash records, causing unprecedented heatwaves across Europe, North America, and South America.

2024.description: El Niño dissipates by mid-year, transitioning rapidly into La Niña. Extreme precipitation events hit parts of Europe and Asia, while high sea surface temperatures trigger heavy monsoons.

2025.description: Established La Niña conditions promote heavy precipitation over Northern South America and Southeast Asia, while bringing drier weather to the southern USA.

2026.description: High solar activity during the solar cycle peak, causing minor magnetosphere warming, alongside neutral ENSO conditions. Stabilized temperature anomalies.
